title: The Challenge of High Resolution
concept: Standard Vision Transformers are too computationally expensive for dense prediction tasks like detection and segmentation due to quadratic self-attention scaling on high-resolution images.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does convolutional projection generate Q, K, and V vectors inside CvT?

options.text: Using depthwise convolutions instead of standard linear layers.

exercises.explain: Correct! Depthwise convolutions project vectors while preserving spatial locality.

cards.title: The resolution wall
cards.body: If you want to classify a 224x224 thumbnail, standard ViT works great. But what if you want to label every pixel in a high-resolution 4K medical scan? Standard self-attention hits a computational 'wall' — the calculations become so massive that even the world's best supercomputers run out of memory.

cards.title: Dense Prediction Challenge
cards.body: Dense prediction tasks (like object detection and semantic segmentation) require outputting predictions at a high spatial resolution. Standard ViT uses global self-attention, meaning its compute cost scales as O(Resolution²), making high-resolution processing virtually impossible.

cards.title: A stadium roll call
cards.body: Imagine a stadium with 50,000 people. If you ask everyone to stand up and shout their name one-by-one, it takes a few hours (linear time). If you ask every single person to walk around and shake hands with every other person in the stadium individually, the stadium will grind to a halt for weeks (quadratic time). Global attention is that stadium handshake.

cards.title: Quadratic memory leak
cards.body: For a 224x224 image, we have 196 patches. For an 896x896 image, the patch count scales to 3,136. The self-attention matrix size grows from 38,416 entries to **9,834,496 entries** — a 256x increase in memory!

cards.title: Not just a parameter problem
cards.body: You might think we can fix this by shrinking the model's layers. However, the bottleneck isn't the number of weights in the layers; it is the size of the attention map generated during the forward pass. Shrinking parameters doesn't stop the attention matrix from exploding.

left.text:
Output: 1 label per image
Low resolution (224x224)
Global context is key

right.text:
Output: Mask/Boxes per pixel
High resolution (896x896+)
Requires local details & speed

exercises.prompt: Why are standard Vision Transformers impractical for high-resolution dense prediction tasks?
exercises.explain: Comparing every pixel patch to every other patch causes memory and computation to explode on high resolutions.

options.text: Their self-attention complexity scales quadratically (O(N²)) with the image resolution
They do not support loss calculation
They are restricted to 10 layers max

exercises.prompt: True or false: Shrinking the model parameter count solves the quadratic memory bottleneck of global attention.
exercises.explain: False. The bottleneck is the size of the attention activation matrix, which is determined by image resolution, not weight counts.
Dense prediction tasks output bounding boxes or pixel labels across the entire spatial layout of the image.
exercises.sentence: Object detection and semantic segmentation are examples of ___ prediction tasks that require high-resolution outputs.

exercises.prompt: Which of these resolutions would cause a standard ViT-Base model to run out of memory on a typical GPU?
exercises.explain: Correct! 2048x2048 creates a sequence of 16,384 tokens, resulting in a massive 268-million-element attention matrix.

exercises.prompt: How can we design a transformer to bypass this quadratic scaling wall?

options.text: By restricting self-attention to local, non-overlapping windows of patches, reducing complexity to linear.
By training the model on low-resolution data and never testing it.
By using random dropout on 99% of the attention matrix.
By converting the transformer into a simple linear regression line.

exercises.explain: Fantastic! Restricting attention to local grids (like Swin does) prevents the quadratic explosion. Next: how local window attention works!
